name: PSR B1620-26 b, the Oldest Known Planet
description: A Jupiter-mass planet orbiting a pulsar and a white dwarf in the globular cluster M4, formed roughly 12.7 billion years ago when the universe was about a billion years old.
significance: Planets existed almost as early as stars did, in a universe with barely any heavy elements to build them from.
dating_method: age of the host globular cluster

name: Globular Clusters Form
description: Dense spheres of hundreds of thousands of stars assembled in the young halo of the forming Milky Way and other galaxies.
significance: The oldest intact structures in the Galaxy, and the clocks astronomers used to put a floor under the age of the universe before the CMB could.

name: HD 140283, the Methuselah Star
description: An extremely metal-poor subgiant 190 light-years away, re-dated in 2021 to 12.01 billion years using MESA stellar models and CHARA interferometry.
significance: Earlier estimates put it older than the universe itself; the revision resolved that embarrassment and left one of the oldest individually dated stars known.
note: 12.01 +/- 0.05 Gyr excluding modelling variance, or 12 +/- 0.5 Gyr including it. Earlier figures near 14.5 Gyr conflicted with the age of the universe.

name: First Rocky Planets
description: Enough silicon, magnesium and iron had been forged by earlier stellar generations for terrestrial planets to condense around the second wave of stars.
significance: The universe only becomes capable of building rocky worlds once stars have died in sufficient numbers — planets like Earth are a second-generation phenomenon.
dating_method: inferred from stellar metallicity and exoplanet host ages

name: Kepler-444: Oldest Known Terrestrial Planets
description: Five sub-Earth-sized planets orbiting an 11.2-billion-year-old star, dated by asteroseismology and announced in 2015.
significance: Earth-sized worlds have existed for over 80% of cosmic history, which widens the window for life by billions of years.

name: Peak of Quasar Activity
description: Supermassive black holes accreted at their most furious rate around redshift 2 to 2.5, lighting up quasars across the young universe.
significance: Black hole growth and star formation peaked together, evidence that the two are physically coupled rather than coincidental.

name: Cosmic Noon: Peak of Star Formation
description: Around redshift 2, roughly 3.5 billion years after the Big Bang, galaxies formed stars about ten times faster than they do today.
significance: The busiest the universe has ever been, and the single most important epoch missing from a timeline that jumps straight from the first galaxies to the Sun.
dating_method: cosmic star formation rate density (Madau & Dickinson 2014)

name: Gaia-Enceladus Merger
description: A dwarf galaxy roughly a quarter of the Milky Way's mass struck it head-on, scattering its stars into the halo and puffing the existing disc into the thick disc.
significance: The last major merger the Galaxy survived, and the reason the Milky Way has a thick disc at all — traced by a 'sausage' of stars in Gaia's velocity data.
dating_method: stellar kinematics and ages from Gaia

name: The Milky Way's Thin Disc Forms
description: Gas settled back into a cold, rotating layer after the Gaia-Enceladus collision, building the thin disc where the Sun and most younger stars now live.
significance: The Sun's future address had to be assembled before the Sun could form in it.
note: The thick-to-thin transition is usually placed near 10 Ga, but stars found in the thin disc since 2024 suggest parts of it may be far older.

name: Cosmic Star Formation Declines
description: Past cosmic noon the star formation rate fell steadily as galaxies exhausted and lost their cold gas supply.
significance: The universe has been winding down for eight billion years; more than half the stars that will ever exist have already formed.

name: Dark Energy Begins to Dominate
description: Around redshift 0.5, the repulsive effect of dark energy overtook the gravitational pull of matter and cosmic expansion stopped slowing and began to accelerate.
significance: The universe changed direction just before the Sun formed — the deceleration-to-acceleration turning point, and the reason the far future is cold and empty rather than collapsing.
dating_method: Type Ia supernova distances; transition observed near z = 0.5
